Cumulative explained variance: [0.72962445 0.95813207 0.99482129 1.        ]

title: Module 2: Deep Dive into Classical Machine Learning
text:
t-SNE: Non-Linear Dimensionality Reduction

The Algorithm
t-Distributed Stochastic Neighbor Embedding (t-SNE) preserves local structure by modeling pairwise similarities.
High-dimensional similarities: \[p_{j|i} = \frac{\exp(-\|x_i - x_j\|^2 / 2\sigma_i^2)}{\sum_{k \neq i} \exp(-\|x_i - x_k\|^2 / 2\sigma_i^2)}\]
Low-dimensional similarities (using t-distribution): \[q_{ij} = \frac{(1 + \|y_i - y_j\|^2)^{-1}}{\sum_{k \neq l} (1 + \|y_k - y_l\|^2)^{-1}}\]
Cost function (KL Divergence): \[KL(P\|Q) = \sum_i \sum_j p_{ij} \log \frac{p_{ij}}{q_{ij}}\]


Key Parameters

Perplexity: Controls the effective number of neighbors (typical: 5-50)
Learning rate: Step size for gradient descent
Early exaggeration: Scales probabilities in early iterations to form tighter clusters



Show the Python Code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from sklearn.manifold import TSNE
from sklearn.decomposition import PCA
from sklearn.datasets import make_swiss_roll

# Generate Swiss Roll dataset (classic non-linear manifold)
X, color = make_swiss_roll(n_samples=1500, noise=0.1, random_state=42)

# Apply PCA
pca = PCA(n_components=2)
X_pca = pca.fit_transform(X)

# Apply t-SNE with different perplexities
perplexities = [10, 30, 50]

fig, axes = plt.subplots(2, 3, figsize=(18, 12))

# Original 3D data (projected to 2D for visualization)
axes[0, 0].scatter(X[:, 0], X[:, 2], c=color, cmap='viridis', s=20, alpha=0.6)
axes[0, 0].set_title('Original Swiss Roll (2D projection)')
axes[0, 0].set_xlabel('X')
axes[0, 0].set_ylabel('Z')

# PCA
axes[0, 1].scatter(X_pca[:, 0], X_pca[:, 1], c=color, cmap='viridis', s=20, alpha=0.6)
axes[0, 1].set_title(f'PCA (2 components)\nVariance explained: {sum(pca.explained_variance_ratio_)*100:.1f}%')
axes[0, 1].set_xlabel('PC1')
axes[0, 1].set_ylabel('PC2')

# t-SNE with default perplexity
tsne = TSNE(n_components=2, perplexity=30, random_state=42, n_iter=1000)
X_tsne = tsne.fit_transform(X)

axes[0, 2].scatter(X_tsne[:, 0], X_tsne[:, 1], c=color, cmap='viridis', s=20, alpha=0.6)
axes[0, 2].set_title('t-SNE (perplexity=30)')
axes[0, 2].set_xlabel('t-SNE 1')
axes[0, 2].set_ylabel('t-SNE 2')

# t-SNE with different perplexities
for idx, perplexity in enumerate(perplexities):
    tsne = TSNE(n_components=2, perplexity=perplexity, random_state=42, n_iter=1000)
    X_tsne_p = tsne.fit_transform(X)
    
    row = 1
    col = idx
    axes[row][col].scatter(X_tsne_p[:, 0], X_tsne_p[:, 1], c=color, cmap='viridis', s=20, alpha=0.6)
    axes[row][col].set_title(f't-SNE (perplexity={perplexity})')
    axes[row][col].set_xlabel('t-SNE 1')
    axes[row][col].set_ylabel('t-SNE 2')

axes[1, 2].axis('off')

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

title: Module 2: Deep Dive into Classical Machine Learning
text:
Cross-Validation

K-Fold Cross-Validation
Divide data into \(K\) folds. Train on \(K-1\) folds, validate on the remaining fold. Repeat \(K\) times.
\[CV_{(K)} = \frac{1}{K} \sum_{k=1}^{K} \text{MSE}_k\]


Stratified Cross-Validation
For classification, ensure each fold has approximately the same class distribution.

title: Module 2: Deep Dive into Classical Machine Learning
text:
Performance Metrics

Classification Metrics








Metric
Formula
Use Case




Accuracy
\(\frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}\)
Balanced classes


Precision
\(\frac{TP}{TP + FP}\)
Minimize false positives


Recall
\(\frac{TP}{TP + FN}\)
Minimize false negatives


F1-Score
\(2 \cdot \frac{\text{Precision} \cdot \text{Recall}}{\text{Precision} + \text{Recall}}\)
Balance precision/recall


AUC-ROC
Area under ROC curve
Overall discriminative ability





Regression Metrics



Metric
Formula




MSE
\(\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2\)


RMSE
\(\sqrt{\text{MSE}}\)


MAE
\(\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}|y_i - \hat{y}_i|\)


R²
\(1 - \frac{\sum(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2}{\sum(y_i - \bar{y})^2}\)





Show the Python Code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from sklearn.model_selection import cross_val_score, learning_curve, validation_curve
from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestClassifier
from sklearn.datasets import make_classification
from sklearn.metrics import confusion_matrix, classification_report, roc_curve, auc

# Generate data
X, y = make_classification(n_samples=1000, n_features=20, n_informative=15, 
                           n_redundant=5, random_state=42)

# Train model
rf = RandomForestClassifier(n_estimators=100, random_state=42)

fig, axes = plt.subplots(2, 3, figsize=(18, 12))

# 1. Cross-validation scores
cv_scores = cross_val_score(rf, X, y, cv=5, scoring='accuracy')
axes[0, 0].bar(range(1, 6), cv_scores, color='steelblue')
axes[0, 0].axhline(y=cv_scores.mean(), color='red', linestyle='--', 
                   label=f'Mean: {cv_scores.mean():.3f} (+/- {cv_scores.std()*2:.3f})')
axes[0, 0].set_xlabel('Fold')
axes[0, 0].set_ylabel('Accuracy')
axes[0, 0].set_title('5-Fold Cross-Validation Scores')
axes[0, 0].legend()
axes[0, 0].set_ylim(0, 1)

# 2. Learning curve
train_sizes, train_scores, val_scores = learning_curve(
    rf, X, y, cv=5, train_sizes=np.linspace(0.1, 1.0, 10),
    scoring='accuracy', n_jobs=-1)

train_mean = np.mean(train_scores, axis=1)
train_std = np.std(train_scores, axis=1)
val_mean = np.mean(val_scores, axis=1)
val_std = np.std(val_scores, axis=1)

axes[0, 1].plot(train_sizes, train_mean, 'o-', color='blue', label='Training')
axes[0, 1].fill_between(train_sizes, train_mean - train_std, train_mean + train_std, alpha=0.15, color='blue')
axes[0, 1].plot(train_sizes, val_mean, 'o-', color='green', label='Validation')
axes[0, 1].fill_between(train_sizes, val_mean - val_std, val_mean + val_std, alpha=0.15, color='green')
axes[0, 1].set_xlabel('Training Set Size')
axes[0, 1].set_ylabel('Accuracy')
axes[0, 1].set_title('Learning Curve')
axes[0, 1].legend()
axes[0, 1].set_ylim(0, 1.05)

# 3. Validation curve (varying n_estimators)
param_range = [10, 25, 50, 100, 200]
train_scores, val_scores = validation_curve(
    RandomForestClassifier(random_state=42), X, y,
    param_name='n_estimators', param_range=param_range,
    cv=5, scoring='accuracy', n_jobs=-1)

train_mean = np.mean(train_scores, axis=1)
train_std = np.std(train_scores, axis=1)
val_mean = np.mean(val_scores, axis=1)
val_std = np.std(val_scores, axis=1)

axes[0, 2].plot(param_range, train_mean, 'o-', color='blue', label='Training')
axes[0, 2].fill_between(param_range, train_mean - train_std, train_mean + train_std, alpha=0.15, color='blue')
axes[0, 2].plot(param_range, val_mean, 'o-', color='green', label='Validation')
axes[0, 2].fill_between(param_range, val_mean - val_std, val_mean + val_std, alpha=0.15, color='green')
axes[0, 2].set_xlabel('Number of Estimators')
axes[0, 2].set_ylabel('Accuracy')
axes[0, 2].set_title('Validation Curve')
axes[0, 2].legend()

# Fit final model for confusion matrix and ROC
rf.fit(X, y)
y_pred = rf.predict(X)
y_prob = rf.predict_proba(X)[:, 1]

# 4. Confusion Matrix
cm = confusion_matrix(y, y_pred)
im = axes[1, 0].imshow(cm, interpolation='nearest', cmap=plt.cm.Blues)
axes[1, 0].set_title('Confusion Matrix')
axes[1, 0].set_xlabel('Predicted')
axes[1, 0].set_ylabel('True')
plt.colorbar(im, ax=axes[1, 0])
thresh = cm.max() / 2.
for i in range(cm.shape[0]):
    for j in range(cm.shape[1]):
        axes[1, 0].text(j, i, format(cm[i, j], 'd'),
                       ha="center", va="center",
                       color="white" if cm[i, j] > thresh else "black")

# 5. ROC Curve
fpr, tpr, _ = roc_curve(y, y_prob)
roc_auc = auc(fpr, tpr)
axes[1, 1].plot(fpr, tpr, color='darkorange', lw=2, label=f'ROC (AUC = {roc_auc:.3f})')
axes[1, 1].plot([0, 1], [0, 1], color='navy', lw=2, linestyle='--')
axes[1, 1].set_xlim([0.0, 1.0])
axes[1, 1].set_ylim([0.0, 1.05])
axes[1, 1].set_xlabel('False Positive Rate')
axes[1, 1].set_ylabel('True Positive Rate')
axes[1, 1].set_title('ROC Curve')
axes[1, 1].legend()

# 6. Feature Importance
importances = rf.feature_importances_
indices = np.argsort(importances)[::-1]
axes[1, 2].bar(range(10), importances[indices[:10]])
axes[1, 2].set_xticks(range(10))
axes[1, 2].set_xticklabels([f'Feature {i}' for i in indices[:10]])
axes[1, 2].set_xlabel('Feature')
axes[1, 2].set_ylabel('Importance')
axes[1, 2].set_title('Top 10 Feature Importances')

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

print("Classification Report:")
print(classification_report(y, y_pred))

title: Module 2: Deep Dive into Classical Machine Learning
text:
Best Practices

Always start with simple models (linear regression, logistic regression) as baselines
Use cross-validation to get reliable performance estimates
Scale your features for algorithms sensitive to feature magnitude (PCA, K-Means, SVM)
Handle missing data appropriately before training
Interpret your models — accuracy isn’t everything; understanding matters
Consider the business context when choosing metrics and models
